description: MMLU, HumanEval, LLM-as-judge, regression suites, and production evaluation practices.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an 'evaluation' (eval) for a language model?
options: To estimate the cost of running the model in production based on token usage metrics
To measure the model's capabilities, accuracy, or behavior on a defined set of tasks or prompts
To compress model weights into a format that is smaller and faster for deployment
To review and approve the model's system prompt for compliance with internal content policies
questions.explanation: Evals quantify what a model can and cannot do. They underpin model selection, development iteration, safety auditing, and regression detection. Without structured evals, changes to a model or prompt cannot be reliably assessed.

questions.question: What does the MMLU benchmark measure?
options: The maximum number of tokens per minute a model can generate under sustained load
Multitask knowledge across 57 academic subjects including STEM, law, medicine, and the social sciences using multiple-choice questions
Minimum memory bandwidth utilization during model inference on a standardized GPU cluster
Multi-modal understanding of language and images in a combined reading-comprehension format
questions.explanation: MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding, Hendrycks et al., 2020) evaluates a model's breadth of world knowledge across 57 subjects with four-option multiple-choice questions. It became a standard benchmark for comparing general capability across frontier models.

questions.question: What does the HumanEval benchmark measure?
options: Human preference for model-generated essays compared with human-written essays, scored by crowdworkers
The quality of a model's conversational emotional intelligence in simulated customer-service dialogues
Functional correctness of Python code: a model writes a function from a docstring and automated tests verify it runs correctly
Whether a model's factual answers are accurate when evaluated against a gold-standard Wikipedia knowledge base
questions.explanation: HumanEval (Chen et al., 2021, OpenAI) contains 164 Python programming problems. The model fills in a function body given a docstring and signature; a test suite then executes the result. The pass@k metric reports how often at least one of k sampled solutions passes all tests.

questions.question: What is 'LLM-as-judge' evaluation?
options: A legal framework in which AI-generated content is automatically reviewed by a separate language model to meet publication standards
Using a capable LLM to score or compare model outputs on subjective criteria — such as helpfulness, coherence, or safety — where automated metrics are insufficient
A real-time moderation system that blocks LLM responses that a secondary model classifies as harmful
Running the same prompt through multiple model versions and selecting the response with the highest cross-entropy score
questions.explanation: LLM-as-judge (Zheng et al., 2023, MT-Bench) uses a capable model to rate or pairwise-rank open-ended responses. It can scale evaluation more cheaply than repeated human review, but it does not automatically reproduce human-quality judgment; position, verbosity, self-preference, and rubric effects must be measured against relevant human judgments.

questions.question: What is a 'regression suite' in the context of LLM development?
options: A set of statistical regression models used to predict model performance on unseen benchmarks from training metrics
A curated collection of test prompts and expected behaviors run automatically after every code or model change to detect performance or safety regressions
A benchmarking framework that ranks models by how well their outputs regress to the statistical mean of human-written text
A tool for rolling back model weights to a previous checkpoint whenever a new fine-tuning run reduces benchmark scores
questions.explanation: A regression suite detects when a change — a prompt update, fine-tuning run, or new model version — breaks previously working behavior. Like software unit tests, regression evals are run in CI/CD pipelines to catch regressions before they reach production.

questions.question: What is the 'pass@k' metric used in code generation evaluation?
options: The probability that the model generates exactly k tokens before stopping due to a stop-sequence trigger
The fraction of benchmark problems for which at least one of k independently sampled completions passes all unit tests
A curriculum metric that measures what percentage of training examples the model can reproduce verbatim after k gradient steps
The number of human-written test cases that a model's output passes, averaged across k benchmark problems
questions.explanation: For each problem, pass@k asks whether at least one of k sampled programs passes the tests. HumanEval samples n programs, executes the tests to count c correct programs, and uses a combinatorial estimator for the probability that a size-k selection contains a correct one; there is no k-squared testing claim.

questions.question: What is 'benchmark contamination' (or 'data contamination')?
options: When adversarial prompts embedded in user traffic corrupt a deployed model's in-context behavior during serving
When evaluation benchmark examples appear in the model's training data, inflating benchmark scores beyond what true generalization would predict
When a benchmark suite contains mislabeled answers that unfairly penalize correct model outputs
When test-set questions are translated or paraphrased by annotators, introducing inconsistencies into the evaluation labels
questions.explanation: If a model's training corpus contains the questions and answers from a benchmark, its scores reflect memorization rather than generalization. Contamination is a growing concern as models train on internet snapshots that may include MMLU, HumanEval, or GSM8K solutions. Frontier labs report contamination analyses in technical reports.

questions.question: What role did Elo-style rating play in early Chatbot Arena model evaluation?
options: A fixed numeric score assigned by domain experts after reviewing a standardized set of 100 model outputs
A chess-inspired method for aggregating many pairwise human-preference votes into relative model ratings
A metric from information theory measuring how closely a model's output distribution matches the reference answer distribution
A cost-per-token pricing index that normalizes model capability against API pricing across different providers
questions.explanation: Chatbot Arena collects anonymous pairwise preferences and historically summarized them with Elo-style relative ratings. Such ratings aggregate the sampled users, prompts, and votes; they are not an objective, task-independent measure of capability, and a platform's statistical ranking method can evolve.

questions.question: What is the 'Goodhart's Law' problem in LLM benchmarking?
options: LLMs trained longer tend to converge to a good performance plateau that cannot be improved regardless of further compute
Once a benchmark becomes the optimization target — through fine-tuning, filtering, or prompting — it ceases to be a reliable measure of the underlying capability it was designed to proxy
Larger language models always perform better on benchmarks, making it impossible to compare models of different sizes fairly
Benchmark leaderboards are unreliable because evaluators disagree on what constitutes a correct answer for open-ended tasks
questions.explanation: Goodhart's Law warns that optimizing directly for a proxy can make it less informative. Training, filtering, or prompt selection aimed at a benchmark can inflate its score without a proportional gain in the broader capability the benchmark was intended to represent; the distortion is a risk, not an inevitable outcome in every case.

questions.question: In production LLM systems, what distinguishes an 'online' eval from an 'offline' eval?
options: Offline evals run without network access, while online evals require an internet-hosted model
Offline evals score text-only tasks, while online evals are reserved for browsing and tool-use tasks
Offline evals use curated or replayed data to gate changes; online evals measure sampled live interactions and production outcomes
Offline evals use automated metrics, while online evals use human reviewers without automated monitoring
questions.explanation: Offline evals (pre-deployment) catch known failure modes against fixed golden sets. Online evals (post-deployment) catch distribution shift, unexpected user behaviors, and silent regressions that no static dataset predicted. A mature eval strategy uses both: offline to gate releases and online to monitor production continuously.
